title: Chapter 11: Episode One
content:
**WHO KILLED MY BEST FRIEND?**
*Episode 1: "The Sound Underneath"*
*Produced and narrated by Maya Reyes*
*Runtime: 38:47*
[SOUND: A heartbeat. Slow. Mechanical. Wrong. It continues for seven full seconds — steady, metronomic, off. Then silence.]

MAYA (NARRATION): My name is Maya Reyes. I'm an audio engineer. I make my living by listening — by finding the sounds that hide inside other sounds. The room tone under the interview. The refrigerator hum leaking into the vocal take. The breath before the confession that tells you more than the words that follow it.
[SOUND: The heartbeat again. One beat. Two. Three. Then gone.]
MAYA (NARRATION): Three weeks ago, I heard something I can't explain. And it changed everything.
[SOUND: A low ambient tone rises — warm, analog, like a memory surfacing. It carries a faint hum of room noise. The sound of a small studio. Someone's apartment. A life lived.]

MAYA (NARRATION): This is a story about my best friend. Her name is Lucy Chen. You might know that name already. She had a podcast — *Luminous with Lucy Chen* — and 1.2 million people subscribed to it. She was the wellness girl. The biohacking girl. The one who made you feel like taking care of yourself was a radical act of joy instead of a chore. She was —
[SOUND: A breath. Maya's. Caught. A micro-pause — 1.3 seconds — then she continues, voice steady again.]
MAYA (NARRATION): She was my person.

MAYA (NARRATION): We met sophomore year at UC Santa Cruz. I was a transfer. She was — Lucy. You know how some people just arrive fully themselves? That was her. She walked into the dining hall on the first day in this oversized yellow sweater, and she sat down across from me, and she said, "You have great bone structure, can I do your eyeliner?" And I said, "I don't know you." And she said, "You will."
[SOUND: A faint, faded laugh — distant, like it's being recalled from years ago rather than recorded.]

MAYA (NARRATION): She was right. I did. Within a week, she was teaching me winged liner in the communal bathroom mirror at midnight. Within a month, we were sharing a dorm room. Within a year, I couldn't remember what my life sounded like without her voice in it.

MAYA (NARRATION): Lucy called me every Thursday night. Even after we graduated. Even after she moved to San Francisco and I moved to Portland and then LA and then back up to the Bay. Every Thursday. Ten PM. No exceptions. She'd call, and she'd talk — about her job, about her terrible dates, about some new supplement she was researching. And I would listen. That was our rhythm. She talked. I listened.
MAYA (NARRATION): The last call was September 12th.
[SOUND: Silence. Five seconds. The ambient tone drops away. Just room noise. Just breath.]

MAYA (NARRATION): She told me she'd found something. Something big. Something she couldn't talk about yet but she was excited. She said the word "luminous" in a way I'd never heard her say it before — not like her brand, not like her podcast tagline. Like she'd discovered what it actually meant. She laughed and said, "Maya, I think I finally get it. I think I finally understand what I've been looking for."
[SOUND: A pause. Two beats.]
MAYA (NARRATION): That was the last time I heard her voice.
On the phone.
MAYA (NARRATION): But not the last time I heard her voice at all.
[SOUND: The ambient tone shifts — cooler now, digital, a faint crackle of compressed audio.]

MAYA (NARRATION): Lucy recorded her final episode of *Luminous* on September 14th. It was a Wellness Wednesday segment. She published it that afternoon. Then she posted to her socials that evening — a photo of a smoothie bowl, of all things, captioned "Nourish to flourish, luminous souls ✨" — and then nothing. No posts. No calls. No texts. Her manager filed the missing persons report on September 17th. The police came by her apartment. Her bed was made. Her phone was gone. Her keys were on the counter. No sign of forced entry. No sign of a struggle. No sign of Lucy.
MAYA (NARRATION): Here's what the police know: Lucy Chen is missing.
Here's what the police have done: nothing.
[SOUND: A hollow, metallic echo — the sound of a file closing. Or a door.]
MAYA (NARRATION): But here's what I know: Lucy left something behind. She left her last recording. And in that recording, underneath her voice, there's something she didn't know was there.
Listen.
[SOUND: Audio clip plays. Lucy Chen's voice — bright, warm, alive, slightly compressed from post-production processing.]

LUCY (CLIP): "Hey luminous souls! Welcome back to Wellness Wednesday! Today we're talking about something EVERYONE gets wrong about biohacking — and I mean everyone, including me, up until about three weeks ago —"
MAYA (NARRATION): That's her. That's my best friend. That's the voice that called me every Thursday at ten. That's the voice that said she'd found something. And underneath it — listen —

[SOUND: The audio clip replays. Lucy's voice is faded, reduced to a whisper of itself. And underneath, rising into audibility: a breathing pattern. Slow. Rhythmic. Mechanical. Inhuman. 0.7 hertz. Forty-two cycles per minute. It does not speed up. It does not slow down. It does not vary. It simply breathes.]

MAYA (NARRATION): That sound is not in the studio. It's not room tone. It's not an air conditioner or a refrigerator or a computer fan. I've isolated it. I've frequency-mapped it. I've compared it against four hundred hours of Lucy's previous recordings — this sound does not exist in any episode before September 14th.
[SOUND: The breathing pattern, isolated. Alone. Four seconds. Then gone.]

MAYA (NARRATION): Something was in that room with her. Or — something was recording through her. Or — something was using the same frequency. I don't know yet. But I know this: Lucy heard it too. She just didn't know she heard it. And she didn't know it would be the last thing she'd ever record.

MAYA (NARRATION): That sound kept me up for a week. I listened to it on loop. I ran it through every filter I have. I stripped away Lucy's voice, the room tone, the compression artifacts, until there was nothing left but that pulsing, mechanical rhythm. And then I stopped listening to the sound — and I started counting.
[SOUND: A slow, deliberate click. Like a metronome being set. Then another. Then a third.]

MAYA (NARRATION): Lucy wore a Lumina smartwatch. It was a gift from the company — she was a brand ambassador. The Lumina watch tracks heart rate continuously. It syncs to the app. And Lucy, because she was Lucy, because she tracked everything, because she believed data was a love letter you wrote to your own body — Lucy's heart rate data was still syncing to her account.
[SOUND: The click of a mouse. A scroll wheel. The faint digital sound of data loading.]
MAYA (NARRATION): Her heart rate data was still syncing *after she disappeared.*
[SOUND: Silence. Three seconds. Let that land.]

MAYA (NARRATION): The last upload from Lucy's Lumina watch was September 14th, at 11:47 PM — roughly eight hours after she published her final podcast episode. Her average resting heart rate was 62 BPM. Completely normal. Completely Lucy. But starting at 10:15 PM that night, the pattern shifts. Not the rate — the *pattern*.

MAYA (NARRATION): I'm an audio engineer. I think in waveforms. And when I looked at Lucy's heart rate graph for that night, I saw something anyone looking at raw numbers would miss. I saw a waveform inside a waveform. Spikes in her heart rate — subtle ones, just three to five beats above baseline — repeating in a deliberate, structured sequence. Long-short-long-long. Short-long-short. Over and over. A pattern embedded in the noise of her body's autonomic nervous system.

MAYA (NARRATION): She was controlling her heart rate. Deliberately. By breathing. By meditating. By whatever technique she could use to spike her pulse on command, for fractions of a second at a time, in a pattern that only makes sense if you map it to an alphabet.
[SOUND: The heart rate data, sonified — a series of tones, short and long, pulsing in sequence. Raw. Clipped. Urgent. Like a signal sent from somewhere deep.]
MAYA (NARRATION): I mapped her heart rate spikes to Morse code.
[SOUND: The sonified pattern again — but this time, with the letters overlaid, spoken by Maya in a flat, controlled voice that is trying very hard not to shake.]
MAYA (NARRATION): A-E-T-E-R-N-A.
[SOUND: A beat.]
MAYA (NARRATION): S-U-B.
[SOUND: A beat.]
MAYA (NARRATION): B.
[SOUND: A beat.]
MAYA (NARRATION): A-L-I-V-E.
[SOUND: The sonified pattern cuts out. Silence. Four full seconds.]
MAYA (NARRATION): My best friend is alive. She's being held underground by a biotech company. And she's using the only tool she has left — her own heartbeat — to ask for help.
MAYA (NARRATION): Aeterna.
[SOUND: The word sits in the mix. Flat. Final. Like a name read off an indictment.]

MAYA (NARRATION): That's what Lucy's heart spelled out. Aeterna. As in Aeterna Therapeutics, the Palo Alto biotech unicorn currently valued at six billion dollars and preparing for an IPO on November 15th. Aeterna, whose flagship consumer product is the Lumina app — the period tracker, the wellness platform, the "feminine health ecosystem" that 1.7 million women have downloaded and connected to their bodies.
MAYA (NARRATION): I don't have a smoking gun. Not yet. What I have are questions. And I think the questions might be more dangerous than the answers.
[SOUND: A low thrum — almost subsonic. Tension building.]

MAYA (NARRATION): Why does a wellness app need your blood type? Why does a period tracker share anonymized data with a biotech therapeutics division? Why does the Lumina terms-of-service agreement — which I have read in its entirety, all 14,300 words — include a clause granting Aeterna "biological material usage rights" for any data collected through their platform? Why has Aeterna filed 847 patents related to hemoglobin synthesis, blood oxygen optimization, and erythrocyte engineering?
MAYA (NARRATION): Eight hundred and forty-seven.
And why — why — has the CEO of Aeterna never appeared on camera?

[SOUND: Audio clip plays. A press conference — the murmur of journalists, camera shutters, the ambient wash of a staged corporate event. Then a voice. Male. Accented — Eastern European, but refined. Decades of education layered over the original phonemes. Smooth. Controlled. Almost musical.]

VLAD (CLIP): "We're building the future of human longevity. Not for the few — for everyone. Aeterna's mission is to ensure that the most transformative advances in biomedical science reach the people who need them most."
[SOUND: The press conference fades. Camera shutters dissolve into a single, resonant tone — low, warning, like a cello string vibrating at a frequency that makes your teeth ache.]

MAYA (NARRATION): The future. That's what they all say. That's what every silicon valley evangelist says when they want you to stop asking what they're building and start dreaming about what it might become. The future. As if the future is a place where consequences don't live. As if the road to it isn't paved with things they don't want you to see.
MAYA (NARRATION): But whose future, Vlad? And at what cost?
[SOUND: A single piano note. Low. Slow decay. The ambient warmth from the opening returns, but thinner now — like sunlight through a window that doesn't open all the way.]

MAYA (NARRATION): I need to tell you something about Lucy. Not about what happened to her — about who she was. Because if I just give you the data, the heart rate, the Morse code, the company name — she becomes a headline. She becomes a true crime statistic. She becomes the thing that happened to her instead of the person she is.

MAYA (NARRATION): Lucy Chen was the funniest person I've ever met. Not joke funny — timing funny. She could pause in a way that made you lose your mind before she delivered the punchline. She once made me laugh so hard at a funeral — my grandmother's funeral, God forgive me — that I choked on a mint and she had to Heimlich me in the church bathroom. She was mortified. I was crying. My cousins thought I was having a breakdown. I was. A laughing breakdown. Because Lucy was there.

MAYA (NARRATION): She was also the most disciplined person I've ever known. She meditated every morning at six. She journaled. She ate clean. She tracked every metric of her body — not out of vanity, not out of obsession, but because she genuinely believed that understanding yourself was the first step to loving yourself. She used to say, "Your body is talking to you all the time, Maya. Most people just don't want to listen."
[SOUND: The heartbeat returns. Slow. But no longer mechanical. Now it sounds human. Faltering. Trying.]

MAYA (NARRATION): She was right. Your body is talking. Lucy's body is talking right now. Through her smartwatch. Through her heartbeat. Through the most primitive, desperate form of communication there is — a rhythm, sent from underground, that says: I'm here. Find me.

[SOUND: The studio ambient fades. What replaces it is close, intimate — the sound of headphones, the slight sibilance of a condenser mic picking up every breath. Maya is no longer narrating. She is speaking directly to the mic. Directly to the listener. Directly to Lucy.]
MAYA: Lucy, if you can hear this — and I think you can — I want you to know: I heard you.
[SOUND: A breath. Steadying.]
MAYA: I heard every beat.
[SOUND: The 0.7Hz breathing pattern — but faint now, in the background, like a promise from a room far away.]
MAYA: And I'm not stopping until I find you.
[SOUND: Four seconds of silence.]

MAYA: And to everyone else listening — if you know anything about Aeterna Therapeutics, about the Lumina app, about what happened to Lucy Chen — contact me. The email is in the show notes. The tip line is in the show notes. I will protect your identity. I will follow every lead. This podcast is the investigation the police won't do. The investigation Aeterna doesn't want anyone to do.
[SOUND: Lucy's voice returns — the clip from before. But stripped of its music, its fade-in, its production polish. Just raw voice. Honest. Present. Gone.]
LUCY (CLIP): "Hey luminous souls..."
MAYA: This is Episode One.
[SOUND: The heartbeat. One pulse. A pause. Then another. Then another — each one slightly stronger, slightly faster, like a heart remembering how to beat.]
MAYA: There will be more.
[SOUND: The heartbeat accelerates. Fades into a resonant, sustained tone — not quite music, not quite silence. Then black.]
*— END OF EPISODE 1 —*
It was 2:14 AM when Maya hit publish.

She sat in her studio — a converted closet in her Oakland apartment, egg-crate foam on the walls, a scarlet RedBola mic arm hovering over her desk like a praying mantis — and watched the upload bar crawl across the screen. The file was 38 minutes and 47 seconds. Four hundred and twelve megabytes. Every second of it mattered.
The bar filled.
**Published.**
She refreshed the hosting platform. The dashboard loaded. Under the episode title — *"Who Killed My Best Friend? Ep. 1: The Sound Underneath"* — the play count sat at zero.
She waited.
Hers. The auto-play preview.
She refreshed.

Her phone buzzed — a text from a number she didn't recognize. Then another. Then five in rapid succession. The episode was auto-sharing to Lucy's old social accounts through a scheduled post Maya had set up weeks ago, when she'd still been editing and hadn't yet believed anyone would listen.

Maya didn't sleep. She sat in her chair and watched the number climb and felt something she hadn't felt since September 12th — the last Thursday, the last call, the last time Lucy's voice was meant for her. She felt the shape of purpose. Sharp-edged and hot. The thing that replaces grief when grief has nowhere left to go.
By dawn, the count would hit 50,000.
Because Lucy Chen had 1.2 million followers, and they'd been desperate for answers for three weeks, and now someone was finally asking the right questions out loud.
The play count ticked upward.
Rising like a heartbeat.
Gaining strength.
